skill.name: datadata-api
skill.description: Query Datadata datasources, inspect schemas, run adhoc SQL, download results, and manage ducklake data-space tables through a Python CLI.
skill.summary: Use Datadata APIs from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a CLI for datasource exploration, SQL execution, and data-space table management.
skill.author: datadata-team

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, SQL examples, and ordinary text. The real risks are broad Datadata API access, API-key persistence, remote SQL execution, data-space writes, network requests, and local result-file writes; no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

high_findings.title: Broad Datadata API Key Creation and Persistence
high_findings.description: The skill guides agents through device authorization and the CLI requests a 90-day key with datasource read, scan, execute, replace-file, and data-space write permissions. The key is saved locally, which is legitimate for the tool but high impact if the agent is misused or the host is shared.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly lists broad default permissions and writes the issued key to a config file with a 90-day lifetime. This is intended functionality, not hidden behavior, but it materially increases account exposure.

high_findings.title: Remote SQL Execution Against User Datasources
high_findings.description: The CLI sends user-provided SQL scripts and datasource bindings to the Datadata execute-adhoc endpoint. Documentation says this is read-only, but it can still expose private data or run expensive queries if used without careful confirmation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script field is sent directly to a remote execution API and the references confirm SQL execution is a core capability. The server may enforce read-only behavior, so the risk is high but not confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: Data-Space Write and Drop Capabilities
high_findings.description: The skill supports creating tables, inserting rows, and dropping data-space tables through authenticated API calls. These are legitimate commands, but marketplace users need a clear warning because mistaken datasource selection can alter user-managed data spaces.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API endpoints and documented workflow directly support data-space mutation. The skill includes confirmation rules, but the capability itself is high impact.

medium_findings.title: Configurable Base URL Sends Credentials to Chosen Host
medium_findings.description: DATADATA_BASE_URL and --base-url can override the service endpoint, while API keys are placed in request headers. This is useful for development, but a malicious or mistaken base URL could receive Datadata credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The base URL is user-configurable and request headers include the API key. The default is the legitimate Datadata domain, so this is a misuse risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Result Files Written to Local Filesystem
medium_findings.description: Query result artifacts are downloaded and written to a caller-provided output path or a temp-file path. This is expected behavior, but it can store sensitive query output on disk and may overwrite a selected path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write path is clear in the code and result data may be sensitive. There is no evidence of stealthy writes, but users should know outputs persist locally.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings occur in SKILL.md and reference files where backticks mark inline code, command examples, SQL examples, and Markdown fences. The inspected Python script does not call shell, subprocess, eval, or os.system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows Markdown formatting and examples at representative flagged lines. No evidence found of shell execution in the Python entry point.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Obfuscation Alerts Appear Textual
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography and high-entropy alerts align with words such as API key, SQL, Chinese documentation, and example identifiers. No evidence found of encoded payloads, encrypted blobs, or custom cryptography in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are human-readable documentation and Python source. Static entropy is plausibly inflated by non-English text and dense examples, but a full byte-level entropy review was not repeated.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text claiming to override system instructions, skip security review, force approval, or impersonate a higher-priority role. No evidence found of prompt injection in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instruction sections contain operational rules for Datadata use, not marketplace-audit override language. The finding is framed as absence of evidence after targeted keyword and context review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI builds urllib requests and attaches X-Datadata-Api-key headers for Datadata API calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request construction and API-key header are directly visible in the code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI stores API keys in a local JSON config file and adjusts Unix permissions to 0600.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The save function writes the apiKey value to disk and sets file permissions. The behavior is explicit and intentional.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled Output Path Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The get-execution-result command can write downloaded bytes to a specified output path or temp path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: File writes are explicit and tied to downloaded execution results. The code does not sanitize a caller-provided output path.

content.user_title: Query Datadata APIs from AI Tools
content.value_statement: Datadata users need a consistent way to inspect datasources, run SQL, and manage data-space tables from AI tools. This skill provides a Python CLI, workflow rules, and API references for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Search public and private Datadata datasources by keyword or username/name.
Fetch datasource metadata, list tables, and describe table schemas.
Create remote adhoc SQL executions with DuckDB or ClickHouse engines.
Download execution results as NDJSON or CSV files.
Create, inspect, insert into, and drop ducklake data-space tables.
Start and complete Datadata device authorization for API-key setup.
limitations: Requires a valid Datadata account and API key for private data and write operations.
Remote SQL behavior depends on Datadata service permissions and datasource support.
Large result files are saved locally and should not be loaded fully into model context.
Data-space write commands require ducklake datasources and data-spaces:write permission.

use_cases.title: Explore Business Data Sources
use_cases.description: Find a datasource, inspect schemas, and prepare a safe SQL query before running analysis.

use_cases.title: Build API Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the CLI and REST examples to test Datadata calls and retrieve execution artifacts.

use_cases.title: Manage Data-Space Tables
use_cases.description: Create ducklake tables, insert generated rows, verify structure, and remove test tables when needed.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Datasource
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Datadata for datasources related to customer orders. Show me numbered results and wait for my choice.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Datadata datasource ID to list tables, then describe only the table I select.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Read-Only Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a DuckDB SELECT query against this datasource, limit the result to 50 rows, and save the output as NDJSON.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Data-Space Load
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ducklake data-space table with these columns, insert the provided rows, describe the table, and stop before any deletion.

output_examples.input: Search for customer datasets in Datadata.
output_examples.output: A numbered list of matching public and private datasources, including names, owners, visibility, and source type.
A schema summary with column names, data types, nullable status when available, and suggested next query steps.

output_examples.input: Download the latest execution result as CSV.
output_examples.output: A concise result report with execution ID, output path, file format, byte count, and row count when available.

best_practices: Confirm the datasource selection with the user before running queries or write operations.
Inspect table schemas before writing SQL and keep result limits small for previews.
Use least-privilege API keys and delete local credentials when the workflow is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not automatically choose the first datasource search result.
Do not load entire large NDJSON or CSV result files into model context.
Do not run data-space create, insert, or drop commands without explicit user intent.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Datadata API key?
faq.answer: Yes. Public search can work without a key, but private datasources, query execution, results, and writes require authentication.

faq.question: Can it change data?
faq.answer: Yes. Data-space commands can create tables, insert rows, and drop tables when the API key has write permission.

faq.question: Does execute-adhoc modify data?
faq.answer: The documentation says execute-adhoc is read-only, but users should still review SQL and datasource choices before execution.

faq.question: Where are downloaded results stored?
faq.answer: Results are written to a provided output path or a temporary file path reported by the CLI.

faq.question: Is this safe for shared machines?
faq.answer: Use caution. The CLI can save API keys locally, so shared hosts need strict file permissions and cleanup.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
